chunks.text: The history of exploration is also the history of humanity being drawn toward places not yet seen.
At the end of the fifteenth century
Columbus crossed the Atlantic and reached an unknown continent.
In the sixteenth century, the fleet of Magellan achieved the first circumnavigation of the world.
Many sailors lost their lives, but their voyage rewrote the map of the world.
In the twentieth century, the stage of exploration shifted from the sea to the land
to the sky, and to the poles.
In 1911, Amundsen of Norway was the first to reach the South Pole.
About a month later, the British team of Scott arrived
but all perished on their return.
It was a story of endurance and sacrifice in a harsh environment.
Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay became the first to stand on the summit of Everest.
Their determination showed how strong the human spirit could be against the most difficult obstacle.
The expedition of Ernest Shackleton is another story still told today.
His ship was trapped in ice, with no rescue in sight
yet he brought every member of his crew back alive.
It was leadership and courage pushed to the extreme.
Today, exploration heads toward the deep sea, outer space
and the limits of the human body.
Why do people try to cross the boundary?
Perhaps because the question itself — what lies beyond — is what makes us human.
